elements.sent-2411-001: Activation functions play a crucial role in introducing non-linearities to deep neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-002: We propose a novel approach to designing activation functions by focusing on their gradients and deriving the corresponding functions through integration.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our work introduces the Expanded Integral of the Exponential Linear Unit (xIELU), a trainable piecewise activation function derived by integrating trainable affine transformations applied on the ELU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-004: Conceptually, xIELU can be viewed as extending ReLU$^2$ to effectively handle negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-005: In experiments with 1.1B parameter Llama models trained on 126B tokens of FineWeb Edu, xIELU achieves lower perplexity compared to both ReLU$^2$ and SwiGLU when matched for the same compute cost and parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-006: The choice of activation function significantly impacts a model's performance, training dynamics, and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-007: Activation functions have progressed from the simple step function \citep{McCulloch1943 to more complex variants like ReLU \citep{nair2010rectified , ELU \citep{clevert2015fast , GELU \citep{hendrycks2016gaussian , and SiLU \citep{hendrycks2016gaussian, ramachandran2017searching, elfwing2017sigmoid .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Higher-order activation functions such as ReLU$^2$ \citep{so2021primer and SwiGLU \citep{shazeer2020glu have since demonstrated improved effectiveness and gained widespread adoption in Large Language Models (LLMs).}}
elements.sent-2411-009: GELU and SiLU have emerged as effective alternatives to ReLU in many deep learning applications, due to their more favorable gradient properties.
elements.sent-2411-010: Unlike ReLU, which entirely suppresses negative inputs, GELU and SiLU provide non-zero gradients for negative inputs, thereby mitigating the "dying ReLU" problem \citep{maas2013rectifier .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Studies have shown that ReLU$^2$ outperforms both GELU and SiLU in LLMs \citep{so2021primer, zhang2024relu2wins .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, ReLU$^2$ also suppresses negative inputs and should inherit the same limitations as ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-013: An intuitive approach to improving ReLU$^2$ is to incorporate non-zero gradients for negative inputs, paralleling the approach taken by GELU and SiLU to improve upon ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-014: Trainable activation functions introduce learnable parameters that allow non-linearities to adapt to specific tasks or datasets, with examples including Swish \citep{ramachandran2017searching , PReLU \citep{he2015delving , and SELU \citep{klambauer2017self .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, these approaches have seen limited adoption in LLMs, as they have not consistently outperformed simpler non-trainable alternatives, despite theoretically enhancing model expressivity.
elements.sent-2411-016: The xSiLU activation function \citep{huang2024expandedgating proposes incorporating trainable parameters to control affine transformations on the gradient.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In contrast to earlier approaches, the use of trainable parameters in xSiLU leads to better results due to its control over the negative gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-018: We propose a novel approach to activation function design that focuses on gradient properties.
elements.sent-2411-019: Rather than designing the activation function directly, we derive it through integration of desired gradient behaviors, leading to our proposed Expanded Integral of the Exponential Linear Unit (xIELU).
elements.sent-2411-020: Our empirical results demonstrate that this combination of gradient properties leads to improved performance over existing activation functions in LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-022: We introduce a novel approach to designing activation functions by focusing on gradient properties and deriving the functions through integration.
elements.sent-2411-023: We introduce xIELU, a novel activation function that outperforms ReLU$^{2}$ and SwiGLU when matched for the same compute cost and parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-024: We demonstrate the effectiveness of trainable activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our analysis shows that xIELU adaptively reduces its non-linearity for higher-level representations deeper in the network.
elements.sent-2411-026: This section provides an overview of the activation functions related to xIELU, which is based on the integral of ELU.
elements.sent-2411-027: The positive component of xIELU behaves similarly to ReLU$^2$, while its negative component incorporates a trainable gradient flow similar to xSiLU.
elements.sent-2411-028: Figure \ref{fig:activation_derivatives provides visualizations of these activation functions and their gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) \citep{clevert2015fast is a piecewise activation function that is continuous and monotonically increasing.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It is bounded below for negative inputs and increasing linearly for positive inputs.
elements.sent-2411-031: Mathematically, ELU is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{ELU}(x) = \begin{cases} x \text{if } x > 0 \alpha(e^x - 1) \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a non-trainable hyperparameter controlling the negative saturation level, typically set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-032: For negative inputs, the gradient is bounded within (0, $\alpha$].
elements.sent-2411-033: Notably, ELU does not allow the gradient to take on negative values, which has been identified as a favorable property \citep{huang2024expandedgating .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We base the gradient of xIELU on the ELU function due to its simple integral expression and empirical effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-035: We explore alternative functions for the positive and negative components in Section \ref{subsection:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-036: ReLU Squared (ReLU$^{2}$) \citep{so2021primer squares positive inputs while maintaining zero output for non-positive inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Its computational efficiency and strong performance in standard MLP blocks have led to widespread adoption in LLMs, alongside SwiGLU in gated MLP blocks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Mathematically, ReLU$^{2}$ is defined as: \begin{equation}\text{ReLU}^2(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 \text{if } x > 0 0 \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} The effectiveness of ReLU$^{2}$ is commonly attributed to its similarity to GLUs, as it is equivalent to ReGLU when the U and V weight matrices are identical.
elements.sent-2411-039: However, the theoretical understanding of GLU variants remains limited \citep{shazeer2020glu .}
elements.sent-2411-040: We offer an alternative interpretation: the effectiveness of ReLU$^{2}$ can be attributed to its linearly increasing gradient for positive inputs.
elements.sent-2411-041: This linear growth in gradient flow allows the network to learn from large activation values more effectively than GELU and SiLU, where gradients are bounded above.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our experiments show that piecewise activation functions with linearly increasing gradients for positive inputs consistently achieve better performance than gradients that are bounded above.
elements.sent-2411-043: ReLU$^{2}$ has the following gradient: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dx}\text{ReLU}^2(x) = \begin{cases} 2x \text{if } x > 0 0 \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} ReLU$^{2}$ inherits ReLU's limitation of suppressing negative inputs, which can lead to "dead" neurons that become permanently inactive during training.
elements.sent-2411-044: We extend ReLU$^{2}$ to address this limitation by allowing non-zero gradient for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-045: Expanded SiLU (xSiLU) \citep{huang2024expandedgating proposes introducing trainable parameters to expand the gradient limits of SiLU from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: This transformation corresponds to expanding the range of sigmoid, the gating function of SiLU, from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$.
elements.sent-2411-047: This modification can improve self-gated activation functions like GELU and SiLU, while also enabling the use of unconventional gating functions like arctangent.
elements.sent-2411-048: The improvement in performance is mainly attributed to the introduction of trainable parameters that control the negative gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-049: The xSiLU function is derived as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{xSiLU}(x) = \int \left(\frac{d}{dx}\text{SiLU}(x) \cdot (1 + 2\alpha) - \alpha\right) \, dx = x \cdot (\sigma(x) \cdot (1 + 2\alpha) - \alpha) \end{split}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a trainable scalar parameter that controls the negative gradient flow by expanding the gradient limits from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$.
elements.sent-2411-050: More generally, this approach involves selecting a base function $g(x)$ for the gradient and deriving a new activation function $f(x)$ by computing the integral of an affine transformation applied to $g(x)$. \begin{equation}f(x) = \int (g(x) \cdot \alpha + \beta) \, dx\end{equation} where $\alpha$ controls the gradient flow, $\beta$ shifts the gradient by a constant value and determines the y-intercept of the gradient, and $C$, the constant of integration, shifts the activation function by a constant value and determines the y-intercept of the activation function, with all parameters chosen as scalars.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/activation_functions.pdf} \caption{Activation Functions} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/derivatives.pdf} \caption{Derivatives} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of activation functions and their gradients. ELU is linear for positive inputs while being bounded below for negative inputs. ReLU$^2$ has a linear gradient for positive inputs and zero gradient for negative inputs. xSiLU introduces a trainable parameter $\alpha$ that expands the gradient limits from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$, with its improvement in performance over SiLU primarily attributed to control over the negative gradient flow.} \label{fig:activation_derivatives}
elements.sent-2411-051: We derive xIELU by integrating trainable affine transformations applied to a modified version of the ELU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-052: We multiply the positive gradient of xIELU by 2 to simplify the derived integral expression.
elements.sent-2411-053: Since ELU is defined piecewise, we derive xIELU's positive and negative components separately by integrating the following gradients: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dx}\text{xIELU}(x) = \begin{cases} 2\alpha_{p} x + \beta_{p} \text{if } x > 0 \alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) + \beta_{n} \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ are trainable parameters controlling the gradient flows for positive and negative inputs respectively, and $\beta_{p}$ and $\beta_{n}$ are trainable parameters determining the y-intercepts of the gradient for positive and negative inputs respectively.
elements.sent-2411-054: For positive inputs, integrating the linear gradient yields a quadratic function: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{xIELU}(x > 0) = \int (2\alpha_{p} x + \beta_{p}) \, dx = \alpha_{p} x^2 + \beta_{p} x + C_{p} \end{split}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{p}$ is a trainable scalar parameter controlling the positive gradient flow.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation} xIELU(x > 0) = \int (2\alpha_{p} x + \beta_{p}) \, dx = \alpha_{p} x^2 + \beta_{p} x + C_{p} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-055: We set $\beta_{p = 0.5$ to match the gradient y-intercept of xIELU with established activation functions like GELU and SiLU.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Setting $C_{p = 0$ ensures the function passes through the origin, maintaining consistency with other activation functions.}
elements.sent-2411-057: For negative inputs, integrating the exponential gradient gives: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{xIELU}(x \leq 0) = \int (\alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) + \beta_{n}) \, dx = \alpha_{n} e^{x} - \alpha_{n}x + \beta_{n} x + C \end{split}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{n}$ is a trainable scalar parameter controlling the negative gradient flow.
elements.sent-2411-058: We set $\beta_{p = \beta_{n = 0.5$ to ensure gradient continuity, and $C_{n = -\alpha_{n}$ to ensure function continuity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This yields the complete expression for xIELU: \begin{equation}\text{xIELU}(x) = \begin{cases} \alpha_{p} x^2 + 0.5x \text{if } x > 0 \alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) - \alpha_{n}x + 0.5x \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: Both xIELU and its gradient compute $(e^x - 1)$ for $x \leq 0$, which can suffer from catastrophic cancellation when $x$ approaches zero from below.
elements.sent-2411-061: To ensure numerical stability during training, specialized functions like torch.expm1() need to be applied for both xIELU and its gradient.
elements.sent-2411-062: Alternatively, imposing an upper bound on negative values (e.g. -1e-6) before computing the exponential can also prevent catastrophic cancellation.
elements.sent-2411-063: We also constrain the values of $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ to improve training stability, introducing a small training overhead that can be precomputed and removed during inference:.
elements.sent-2411-064: A basic implementation of xIELU is provided in Appendix \ref{xielu-implementation .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/xielu_positive.pdf} \caption{xIELU Positive Component} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/xielu_positive_derivative.pdf} \caption{xIELU Positive Component Gradient} \end{subfigure} \vskip\baselineskip \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/xielu_negative.pdf} \caption{xIELU Negative Component} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/xielu_negative_derivative.pdf} \caption{xIELU Negative Component Gradient} \end{subfigure} \caption{Visualization of xIELU activation function and its gradients. The parameters $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ control the gradient flow for the positive and negative components, respectively. Higher values of either parameter increase the non-linearity of the xIELU function. For negative inputs, the gradient is bounded within the range ($\beta_{n}-\alpha_{n}, \beta_{n}$]. The choice of $\beta_{p}=\beta_{n}=0.5$ ensures gradient continuity at the point ($0, 0.5$), while setting $C_{p}=0$ and $C_{n}=-\alpha_{n}$ guarantees function continuity at the origin.} \label{fig:xielu_components}

elements.sent-2411-071: SiLU requires one exponentiation, two multiplications, one addition, and one division.

elements.sent-2411-091: Both parameters demonstrate a decreasing trend in deeper layers, indicating that xIELU adaptively reduces its non-linearity when handling higher-level representations.
elements.sent-2411-092: This progression towards simpler transformations for higher-level representations parallels design choices in hierarchical vision architectures \citep{szegedy2014goingdeeperconvolutions, simonyan2015deepconvolutionalnetworkslargescale, he2015deepresiduallearningimage , where such behavior is typically enforced through architectural constraints such as dimensionality reduction.}
elements.sent-2411-093: Notably, xIELU learns this pattern naturally during training without relying on inductive biases.
elements.sent-2411-094: This produces positive activation outputs for negative inputs, which may enhance signal propagation through deeper layers.
elements.sent-2411-095: In contrast, standard activation functions handle negative inputs differently: ReLU outputs zero, GELU and SiLU converge to zero, and ELU converges to a fixed negative value.
elements.sent-2411-096: The generation of positive outputs from negative inputs has precedent in gated architectures, as seen in GEGLU and SwiGLU when both gate and input are negative.
elements.sent-2411-097: We conducted experiments using a simple initialization of $\alpha_{p =\alpha_{n =0.8$ across all layers for xIELU.}}

elements.sent-2411-101: To better understand xIELU's design choices and validate our approach, we conducted ablation experiments using Llama models trained from scratch on 4B tokens from the FineWeb Edu dataset processed with the Meta Llama 3 tokenizer \citep{dubey2024llama3herdmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-102: For our ablation analysis, we used 1.1B parameter models with sequence length 1024 and batch size of 82K tokens.
elements.sent-2411-103: The learning rate follows a combined schedule of linear warmup and cosine decay from 6e-4 to 6e-5.
elements.sent-2411-104: Detailed hyperparameter settings are provided in Appendix \ref{appendix:experiment-setup .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Ablation results are presented in Table \ref{tab:ablation-study .}
elements.sent-2411-106: For the positive component, a linearly increasing gradient achieves the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-107: Activation functions with gradients bounded above, such as SiLU and xSiLU, underperform, likely due to their limited capacity to propagate gradient information across layers.
elements.sent-2411-108: Conversely, higher-order functions like x$^3$, with quadratically increasing gradients, also exhibit inferior performance.
elements.sent-2411-109: We hypothesize that these excessively steep gradients can destabilize training and hinder convergence.
elements.sent-2411-110: For the negative component, a trainable negative gradient flow based on the exponential function achieves the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-111: Attempts to extend ReLU$^2$ with the negative component of SiLU worsens performance compared to ReLU$^2$ alone.
elements.sent-2411-112: In contrast, extending ReLU$^2$ with the negative component of xSiLU leads to improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-113: Switching from an xSiLU-based to an exponential-based trainable negative gradient flow further improves performance.
elements.sent-2411-114: We hypothesize that this improvement arises from the monotonically increasing nature of the gradient of the exponential function.
elements.sent-2411-115: While xIELU demonstrates promising results, there are several important limitations to consider.
elements.sent-2411-116: First, our current implementation is slower than highly optimized implementations of established activation functions, though we expect this gap can be reduced through CUDA kernel fusion and optimization techniques.
elements.sent-2411-117: Second, while our experiments with 1.1B parameter models provide evidence for xIELU's effectiveness, we have yet to validate these results at larger scales.
elements.sent-2411-118: Testing with larger models across different architectures and tasks would help establish how xIELU's benefits generalize.
elements.sent-2411-119: Third, without true zeros in its outputs, xIELU lacks activation sparsity \citep{mirzadeh2023relustrikesbackexploiting, song2024prosparseintroducingenhancingintrinsic and its associated computational benefits.}
elements.sent-2411-120: In this work, we introduced a novel approach to activation function design by explicitly focusing on gradient properties and deriving functions through integration.
elements.sent-2411-121: This methodology led to the development of xIELU, which combines the beneficial gradient characteristics of existing activation functions: the linearly increasing gradient of ReLU² for positive inputs and trainable negative gradient flow inspired by xSiLU for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-122: Our empirical results with 1.1B parameter Llama models demonstrate that xIELU achieves superior performance compared to both ReLU² and SwiGLU while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-123: The effectiveness of xIELU suggest that focusing on gradient properties is a promising direction for developing new activation functions.

elements.sent-2411-125: Basic implementation of xIELU using PyTorch.
elements.verbatim-2411-001: import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class XIELU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, alpha_p_init=0.8, alpha_n_init=0.8, beta=0.5, max_clip=-1e-6): super(XIELU, self).__init__() self.beta = beta self.alpha_p = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.tensor(alpha_p_init)) - 1) self.alpha_n = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.tensor(alpha_n_init - self.beta)) - 1) self.max_clip = torch.tensor(max_clip) def forward(self, x): alpha_p = F.softplus(self.alpha_p) alpha_n = self.beta + F.softplus(self.alpha_n) return torch.where(x > 0, alpha_p * x * x + self.beta * x, alpha_n * torch.expm1(torch.max(x, self.max_clip)) - alpha_n * x + self.beta * x)

Visualization of xIELU and its derivatives for a 1.1B parameter model trained on 126B tokens. The earlier layers (darker colors) show increased non-linearity and later layers (lighter colors) showing decreased non-linearity.} \label{fig:xielu_appendix_combined}
elements.verbatim-2411-002: import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class XIELU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, alpha_p_init=0.8, alpha_n_init=0.8, beta=0.5, eps=-1e-6): super(XIELU, self).__init__() self.beta = beta self.alpha_p = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.tensor(alpha_p_init)) - 1) self.alpha_n = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.tensor(alpha_n_init - self.beta)) - 1) self.eps = torch.tensor(eps) def forward(self, x): alpha_p = F.softplus(self.alpha_p) alpha_n = self.beta + F.softplus(self.alpha_n) return torch.where(x > 0, alpha_p * x * x + self.beta * x, alpha_n * torch.expm1(torch.min(x, self.eps)) - alpha_n * x + self.beta * x)
elements.sent-2411-127: Our work proposes a novel approach to designing activation functions by focusing on their gradients and deriving the corresponding activation functions using integration.
elements.sent-2411-128: We introduce the Expanded Integral of the Exponential Linear Unit (xIELU), a trainable piecewise activation function derived by integrating trainable affine transformations applied to the Exponential Linear Unit (ELU).
elements.sent-2411-129: Conceptually, xIELU can be viewed as an extension of ReLU$^2$ to handle negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-130: The trainable parameters in xIELU allow it to adaptively reduce its nonlinearity for higher-level representations deeper in the network.
elements.sent-2411-131: In experiments with 1.1B and 3B parameter Llama models trained on 125B tokens of FineWeb Edu, xIELU achieves lower perplexity compared to popular activation functions like ReLU$^2$ and SwiGLU when matched for the same compute cost and parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-132: A reference implementation is available at \href{https://github.com/Anonymous5823/xielu {https://github.com/Anonymous5823/xielu .}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Activation functions play a crucial role in introducing nonlinearities to deep neural networks and significantly impact model performance, training dynamics, and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-134: The evolution of activation functions has progressed from the simple step function \citep{McCulloch1943 to more sophisticated variants like ReLU \citep{nair2010rectified , ELU \citep{clevert2015fast , GELU \citep{hendrycks2016gaussian , and SiLU \citep{hendrycks2016gaussian, ramachandran2017searching, elfwing2017sigmoid .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Higher-order activation functions, such as ReLU$^2$ \citep{so2021primer and SwiGLU \citep{shazeer2020glu , have since demonstrated superior effectiveness and gained widespread adoption in Large Language Models (LLMs) \cite{chowdhery2022palmscalinglanguagemodeling, touvron2023llama2openfoundation, dubey2024llama3herdmodels .}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: GELU and SiLU have established themselves as superior alternatives to ReLU in numerous deep learning applications, primarily due to their gradient properties that allow learning from negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-137: While ReLU has zero gradient for negative inputs, GELU and SiLU provide nonzero gradients for negative inputs, thereby mitigating the "dying ReLU" problem \citep{maas2013rectifier .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Recent studies have shown that ReLU$^2$ outperforms both GELU and SiLU in LLMs \citep{so2021primer, zhang2024relu2wins .}
elements.sent-2411-139: However, ReLU$^2$ also has zero gradient for negative inputs and should inherit the same limitations as ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-140: An intuitive approach to improving ReLU$^2$ is to incorporate nonzero gradients for negative inputs, paralleling the approach taken by GELU and SiLU to improve ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-141: Trainable activation functions incorporate learnable parameters to enable adaptation to specific tasks or datasets.
elements.sent-2411-142: Notable examples include PReLU \citep{he2015delving , SELU \citep{klambauer2017self and Swish \citep{ramachandran2017searching .}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: However, trainable activation functions have seen limited adoption in practice, as they have not consistently outperformed simpler non-trainable alternatives, despite theoretically enhancing model expressivity.
elements.sent-2411-144: In contrast, the use of trainable parameters in xSiLU \citep{huang2024expandedgating demonstrates improvements over SiLU.}
elements.sent-2411-145: They attribute the effectiveness of GELU and SiLU over ReLU to the gradient being able to take on negative values.
elements.sent-2411-146: Motivated by these insights into how gradients influence the performance of activation functions, we propose a new approach to designing activation functions that prioritizes gradient behavior rather than the activation function itself.
elements.sent-2411-147: By integrating desired gradient properties, we derive the Expanded Integral of the Exponential Linear Unit (xIELU), which combines the strengths of existing approaches: a trainable and linearly increasing gradient for positive inputs based on ReLU$^2$ and a trainable gradient that can take negative values for negative inputs based on xSiLU.
elements.sent-2411-148: Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that this combination of gradient properties yields superior performance compared to existing activation functions used in LLMs.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/activation_functions.pdf} \caption{Activation Functions} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/derivatives.pdf} \caption{Derivatives} \end{subfigure} \caption{Comparison of activation functions related to xIELU and their gradients. ELU is linearly increasing for positive inputs and is bounded below for negative inputs. ReLU$^2$ has a linearly increasing gradient for positive inputs and zero gradient for negative inputs. xSiLU introduces a trainable parameter $\alpha$ that controls the magnitude and range of negative-valued gradients by expanding the gradient limits of SiLU from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$.} \label{fig:activation_derivatives}
elements.sent-2411-150: We propose a novel approach to designing activation functions by focusing on gradient properties and deriving the activation functions using integration.
elements.sent-2411-151: We introduce xIELU, a trainable activation function that outperforms ReLU$^2$ and SwiGLU when matched for the same compute cost and parameter count.
elements.sent-2411-152: We demonstrate the effectiveness of trainable activation functions, showing that xIELU adaptively reduces its nonlinearity for higher-level representations deeper in the network.
elements.sent-2411-153: This section provides an overview of the activation functions related to xIELU.
elements.sent-2411-154: Figure \ref{fig:activation_derivatives illustrates ELU, ReLU$^{2}$, and xSiLU and their respective gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Exponential Linear Unit (ELU) \citep{clevert2015fast is a continuous, monotonically increasing, and piecewise activation function that is linearly increasing for positive inputs and bounded below for negative inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The ELU activation function is expressed as: \begin{equation}\text{ELU}(x) = \begin{cases} x \text{if } x > 0 \alpha(e^x - 1) \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a non-trainable hyperparameter controlling the negative saturation level, typically set to 1.
elements.sent-2411-157: We choose to base the gradient of xIELU on the ELU activation function solely due to its computationally efficient integral and empirical effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-158: We investigate alternative bounded and unbounded functions for both the positive and negative gradient components in Section \ref{subsection:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Squared ReLU (ReLU$^{2}$) \citep{so2021primer squares positive inputs while zeroing out negative inputs.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Due to its computational efficiency and strong empirical performance, it has seen widespread adoption in LLMs alongside SwiGLU.
elements.sent-2411-161: The ReLU$^{2}$ activation function is expressed as: \begin{equation}\text{ReLU}^2(x) = \begin{cases} x^2 \text{if } x > 0 0 \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} The effectiveness of ReLU$^{2}$ is commonly attributed to its similarity with Gated Linear Units (GLUs), as it is equivalent to ReGLU when the U and V weight matrices are identical.
elements.sent-2411-162: However, due to limited theoretical understanding of GLU variants \citep{shazeer2020glu , the underlying reasons for ReLU$^{2}$ effectiveness also remain unclear.}
elements.sent-2411-163: We propose an explanation for ReLU$^{2}$ effectiveness based on its gradient behavior: unlike GELU and SiLU which have gradients that are bounded above for positive inputs, ReLU$^{2}$ exhibits linearly increasing gradients for positive inputs, which enables more effective learning from large activation values.
elements.sent-2411-164: Our experiments show that piecewise activation functions with linearly increasing gradients for positive inputs consistently achieve better performance than those with gradients that are bounded above.
elements.sent-2411-165: The gradient of ReLU$^{2}$ is expressed as: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dx}\text{ReLU}^2(x) = \begin{cases} 2x \text{if } x > 0 0 \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} ReLU$^{2}$ inherits ReLU property of zero gradient for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-166: This prevents learning from negative inputs and can cause hidden units to become permanently inactive during training, limiting network capacity.
elements.sent-2411-167: Our work extends ReLU$^{2}$ to address this limitation by allowing nonzero gradients for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-168: The presence of negative-valued gradients is crucial for activation function performance \cite{huang2024expandedgating .}
elements.sent-2411-169: While ELU and ATLU \cite{huang2024expandedgating have gradients defined for negative inputs, these gradients are strictly positive, resulting in performance that are only marginally better than ReLU.}
elements.sent-2411-170: In contrast, GELU and SiLU achieve better performance through their negative-valued gradients, which arise from the negative turning point in their gradient functions.
elements.sent-2411-171: By introducing trainable parameters that control affine transforms on the gradients, enhanced versions of ELU and ATLU can gain negative-valued gradients and surpass GELU and SiLU.
elements.sent-2411-172: This technique can also be applied to further enhance GELU and SiLU.
elements.sent-2411-173: Expanded SiLU (xSiLU) \citep{huang2024expandedgating improves SiLU by introducing trainable parameters to expand the gating range of sigmoid, the gating function of SiLU, and the gradient limits of SiLU from $(0, 1)$ to $(-\alpha, 1+\alpha)$.}
elements.sent-2411-174: This gradient-based design approach can be abstracted to selecting a base function $g(x)$ for the gradient and deriving a new trainable activation function $f(x)$ by taking the integral of trainable affine transformations applied to $g(x)$: \begin{equation}f(x) = \int (\alpha \cdot g(x) + \beta) \, dx\end{equation} where $\alpha$ controls the magnitude and range of the gradient.
elements.sent-2411-175: The constant of integration $C$ shifts the activation function by a constant value and determines the y-intercept of the activation function.
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{subfigure}{0.47\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/xielu_positive.pdf} \caption{xIELU$(x>0)$} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/xielu_positive_derivative.pdf} \caption{xIELU$(x>0)$ Gradient} \end{subfigure} \vskip\baselineskip \begin{subfigure}{0.47\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/xielu_negative.pdf} \caption{xIELU$(x\leq0)$} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.47\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/xielu_negative_derivative.pdf} \caption{xIELU$(x\leq0)$ Gradient} \end{subfigure} \caption{Visualization of xIELU and its gradients. The parameters $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ control the magnitude and range of the gradients. Larger values of either parameter increase the nonlinearity of xIELU. For the positive component, constraining $\alpha_{p}>0$ ensures a linearly increasing gradient. For the negative component, the gradient is bounded within the range $(\beta_{n}-\alpha_{n}, \beta_{n}]$ and constraining $\alpha_{n}>\beta_{n}$ ensures the presence of negative-valued gradients.} \label{fig:xielu_components}
elements.sent-2411-176: We derive xIELU by taking the integral of trainable affine transformations applied to the ELU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-177: The positive gradient of xIELU is multiplied by 2 to simplify the derived integral expression.
elements.sent-2411-178: As ELU is a piecewise activation function, we derive the positive and negative components for xIELU separately by integrating the following gradients: \begin{equation}\frac{d}{dx}\text{xIELU}(x) = \begin{cases} 2\alpha_{p} x + \beta_{p} \text{if } x > 0 \alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) + \beta_{n} \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases} \label{eq:xielu_gradient}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ is a trainable scalar parameter learned independently for each layer.
elements.sent-2411-179: The subscripts $p$ and $n$ denote values for positive and negative components respectively.
elements.sent-2411-180: For the positive component of xIELU, integrating the linearly increasing gradient gives: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{xIELU}(x > 0) = \int (2\alpha_{p} x + \beta_{p}) \, dx = \alpha_{p} x^2 + \beta_{p} x + C_{p} \end{split}\end{equation} To match the gradient properties of xIELU with established activation functions like GELU and SiLU, we set $\beta_{p = 0.5$ to match the y-intercept of the gradient and $C_{p = 0$ to ensure xIELU passes through the origin.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: For the negative component of xIELU, integrating the exponential gradient gives: \begin{equation}\begin{split} \text{xIELU}(x \leq 0) = \int (\alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) + \beta_{n}) \, dx = \alpha_{n} e^{x} - \alpha_{n}x + \beta_{n} x + C_{n} \end{split}\end{equation} We set $\beta_{p = \beta_{n = 0.5$ to ensure gradient continuity and $C_{n = -\alpha_{n}$ to ensure function continuity through the origin.}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Maintaining both function and gradient continuity is desirable for facilitating smooth training dynamics and stable gradient descent updates during training.
elements.sent-2411-183: Combining these components yields the complete expression for xIELU: \begin{equation}\text{xIELU}(x) = \begin{cases} \alpha_{p} x^2 + 0.5x \text{if } x > 0 \alpha_{n} (e^{x} - 1) - \alpha_{n}x + 0.5x \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases} \label{eq:xielu_function}\end{equation} where $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ are trainable scalar parameters learned independently for each layer.
elements.sent-2411-184: We constrain the ranges of values $\alpha_{p}$ and $\alpha_{n}$ can take to ensure favorable gradient properties for xIELU.
elements.sent-2411-185: This introduces minimal training overhead, which can be eliminated during inference:.
elements.sent-2411-186: To constrain $\alpha_{p > 0$, we apply the softplus function to $\alpha_{p}$.}
elements.sent-2411-187: This ensures a linearly increasing gradient for positive inputs.
elements.sent-2411-188: To constrain $\alpha_{n > \beta_{n}$, we add $\beta_{n}$ to the softplus of $\alpha_{n}$.}
elements.sent-2411-189: This ensures the presence of negative-valued gradients for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-190: Figure \ref{fig:xielu_components illustrates the positive and negative components of xIELU alongside their respective gradients.}
elements.sent-2411-191: Both xIELU and its gradient compute $(e^x - 1)$ for the negative component (Equations \ref{eq:xielu_gradient , \ref{eq:xielu_function ).}}
elements.sent-2411-192: This operation can suffer from catastrophic cancellation as $x$ approaches zero from below, leading to significant precision loss \citep{goldberg1991computer .}
elements.sent-2411-193: To ensure numerical stability, specialized functions like torch.expm1() need to be applied to both xIELU and its gradient.
elements.sent-2411-194: Alternatively, imposing a small upper bound (e.g. -1e-6) on negative inputs before computing the exponential can also address the issue.
elements.sent-2411-195: A reference implementation is provided in Appendix \ref{xielu-implementation .}
elements.sent-2411-196: We do not encounter stability issues training models using xIELU with BF16 precision, but further analysis will be necessary for lower precision training \citep{fishman2024scalingfp8trainingtrilliontoken .}

elements.sent-2411-197: In comparison, the tanh-based approximation of GELU requires two exponentiations, six multiplications, four additions, and one division.
elements.sent-2411-198: Since exponentiation is computationally more expensive than basic arithmetic operations, xIELU’s single exponentiation makes it theoretically as efficient as SiLU and more efficient than GELU.
elements.sent-2411-199: We also introduce the Expanded Integral of PReLU (xIPReLU), a computationally efficient alternative to xIELU, derived by taking the integral of trainable affine transformations applied to PReLU (derivation provided in Appendix \ref{appendix:xIPReLU ).}
elements.sent-2411-200: As shown in Table \ref{tab:time-per-iteration , our PyTorch implementations for xIELU and xIPReLU relying on torch.compile() for optimization exhibit performance below theoretical expectations compared to established activation functions with optimized CUDA implementations.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Custom CUDA kernels with fusion optimization would likely bridge this performance gap for xIELU and xIPReLU.
elements.sent-2411-202: Similar to GELU and SiLU, xIELU and xIPReLU compute their quadratic and exponential terms on the fly without persistent storage.
elements.sent-2411-203: While their piecewise nature involve conditional checks, modern GPU architectures efficiently handle such branching.
elements.sent-2411-204: Memory requirements for xIELU and xIPReLU are comparable to those of established activation functions, as they involve similar storage needs for computing and retaining activations and gradients during training.
elements.sent-2411-205: We conduct experiments on the task of autoregressive language modeling using the common decoder-only transformer architecture \citep{vaswani2017attentionneed , based on Llama \citep{touvron2023llama2openfoundation , with alternating attention and multilayer perceptron (MLP) blocks.}}
elements.sent-2411-206: The model employs RMSNorm \citep{zhang2019rootmeansquarelayer for normalization and Rotary Position Embeddings \citep{su2023roformerenhancedtransformerrotary for positional encoding.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: We use standard multihead attention instead of grouped-query attention \citep{ainslie2023gqatraininggeneralizedmultiquery .}
elements.sent-2411-208: For our comparative analysis, the only modification introduced is to the activation function used within the MLP block.
elements.sent-2411-209: We focus on comparing the performance of xIELU and xIPReLU with the state-of-the-art activation functions ReLU$^2$ and SwiGLU.
elements.sent-2411-210: SwiGLU is implemented within a gated MLP block, while xIELU, xIPReLU and ReLU$^2$ are implemented within standard MLP blocks.
elements.sent-2411-211: To ensure comparable compute costs and parameter counts across activation functions, the hidden dimension of standard MLP blocks is scaled by a factor of 1.5 compared to gated MLP blocks.

elements.sent-2411-220: The effectiveness of xIELU and xIPReLU validate our approach to activation function design by focusing on favorable gradient properties.
elements.sent-2411-221: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:loss , xIELU and xIPReLU initially underperform during the early stages of training, with higher perplexity values compared to SwiGLU.}
elements.sent-2411-222: However, they match SwiGLU after training on 20B tokens and shows increasing performance gains throughout the rest of training, suggesting potential for further improvements with extended training durations.
elements.sent-2411-223: For 3B parameter models, xIELU maintains its advantage over SwiGLU, demonstrating the potential for scalability across larger model sizes.

elements.sent-2411-225: Both parameters demonstrate a consistent decreasing trend in deeper layers, indicating that xIELU adaptively reduces its nonlinearity when handling higher-level representations.

elements.sent-2411-230: We evaluate the performance of SwiGLU and xIELU activation functions in 3B-parameter Llama models across a diverse suite of downstream tasks, as summarized in Table \ref{tab:model-comparison .}
elements.sent-2411-231: While the aggregate scores (39.6 for SwiGLU and 39.7 for xIELU) suggest comparable overall performance at the 3B scale, we observe distinct task-specific strengths for each activation function.
elements.sent-2411-232: SwiGLU performs better on tasks requiring linguistic understanding and commonsense reasoning, achieving higher scores on benchmarks such as Winogrande, SIQA, and HellaSwag, along with marginal improvements on OpenBookQA and CommonsenseQA.
elements.sent-2411-233: These results indicate that SwiGLU may be more effective for tasks involving contextual interpretation and inference based on general knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-234: In contrast, xIELU performs better on tasks involving factual recall and structured reasoning, achieving higher scores on benchmarks such as PIQA, TriviaQA, and GSM8k, along with marginal improvements on ARC.
elements.sent-2411-235: The improved performance in factual recall tasks may be attributed to the larger hidden dimensions of standard MLPs compared to gated MLPs, which may provide greater capacity for information storage \citep{allenzhu2024physicslanguagemodels33 .}
elements.sent-2411-236: Similarly, the adaptive nature of xIELU, which enables nonlinear transformations of varying complexity across network depth, may contribute to its improved performance in reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-237: These findings suggest that xIELU may be more suited for precise information retrieval and logical problem-solving.

elements.sent-2411-238: We evaluate key design choices of xIELU and xIPReLU through ablation studies on 1.1B parameter Llama models trained from scratch on 4B tokens from the FineWeb Edu dataset using the Meta Llama 3 tokenizer \citep{dubey2024llama3herdmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-239: Training is conducted with a sequence length of 1024, a batch size of 82K tokens, and a learning rate schedule that includes linear warmup followed by cosine decay, starting at 6e-4 and decreasing to 6e-5.
elements.sent-2411-240: Hyperparameter settings are provided in Appendix \ref{appendix:experiment-setup2 .}
elements.sent-2411-241: The results of the ablation studies are presented in Table \ref{tab:ablation-study .}
elements.sent-2411-242: For the positive component, a linearly increasing gradient provides the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-243: Activation functions with gradients that are bounded above, such as SiLU or xSiLU, underperform, likely due to their reduced ability to propagate gradient information.
elements.sent-2411-244: Conversely, higher-order functions like x$^3$, which exhibit quadratically increasing gradients, also demonstrate inferior performance.
elements.sent-2411-245: This is likely due to the excessively steep gradients destabilizing training and hindering convergence.
elements.sent-2411-246: For the negative component, a trainable gradient capable of taking negative values provides the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-247: Naively combining a linearly increasing gradient for the positive component with an untrainable negative component, such as SiLU, degrades performance compared to ReLU$^2$ .
elements.sent-2411-248: Extending with a trainable negative component, like xSiLU, already improves performance over ReLU$^2$.
elements.sent-2411-249: However, the best results appear to be achieved when using trainable negative components that are monotonically increasing, such as the exponential-based gradient used in xIELU and the linear-based gradient used in xIPReLU.
elements.sent-2411-250: For the gradient y-intercept, setting $\beta_{p =\beta_{n =0.5$ provides the best performance.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Attempting to use a trainable parameter for $\beta_{p =\beta_{n}$ provides no improvement, likely because the gradient y-intercept can indirectly be controlled through the weight scale in the output projection layer.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Setting $\beta_{p =\beta_{n =0$ degrades performance, presumably because the activation output distribution becomes constrained to positive values.}}
elements.sent-2411-253: While our approach to designing activation functions yields promising results, there are several important limitations to consider: First, the current implementations of xIELU and xIPReLU exhibit performance below their theoretical capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-254: Custom CUDA kernel fusion and optimization techniques will be necessary to bridge this performance gap.
elements.sent-2411-255: Second, while our experiments on 1.1B and 3B-parameter models demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, validation on larger-scale models and different architectures remains necessary.
elements.sent-2411-256: Higher-order activation functions like xIELU and xIPReLU may also require additional modifications to support training at lower precisions \citep{fishman2024scalingfp8trainingtrilliontoken .}
elements.sent-2411-257: Third, although analyzing gradient properties can explain the relative effectiveness of various activation functions (summary provided in Appendix \ref{effectiveness ), and combining favorable gradient properties enables the development of effective activation functions, a more rigorous framework is needed to fully understand why certain gradient properties, such as the ability to take negative values, are favorable.}
elements.sent-2411-258: In this work, we introduce a novel approach to activation function design by explicitly focusing on gradient properties and deriving the corresponding activation functions using integration.
elements.sent-2411-259: This leads to the development of xIELU and xIPReLU, which combine the beneficial gradient properties of existing activation functions: the linearly increasing gradient of ReLU² for positive inputs and a trainable gradient that can take negative values like xSiLU for negative inputs.
elements.sent-2411-260: Our empirical results demonstrate that xIELU and xIPReLU achieve better performance than existing state-of-the-art activation functions like ReLU$^2$ and SwiGLU.
elements.sent-2411-261: The effectiveness of xIELU and xIPReLU suggests that primarily focusing on gradient properties is a promising direction for designing novel activation functions.

elements.verbatim-2411-003: import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class XIELU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, alpha_p_init=0.8, alpha_n_init=0.8, beta=0.5, eps=-1e-6): super(XIELU, self).__init__() self.beta = beta self.alpha_p = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.exp(alpha_p_init) - 1)) self.alpha_n = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.exp(alpha_n_init - self.beta) - 1)) self.eps = torch.tensor(eps) def forward(self, x): alpha_p = F.softplus(self.alpha_p) alpha_n = self.beta + F.softplus(self.alpha_n) return torch.where(x > 0, alpha_p * x * x + self.beta * x, alpha_n * torch.expm1(torch.min(x, self.eps)) - alpha_n * x + self.beta * x)

elements.sent-2411-268: The Expanded Integral of PReLU (xIPReLU) is derived by taking the integral of trainable affine transforms applied to the PReLU activation function.
elements.sent-2411-269: The notation and methodology used to derive xIPReLU mirrors that of xIELU in Section \ref{methodology .}
elements.sent-2411-270: This yields the following expression for xIPReLU: \begin{equation}\text{xIPReLU}(x) = \begin{cases} \alpha_{p} x^2 + 0.5x \text{if } x > 0 \alpha_{n} x^2 + 0.5x \text{if } x \leq 0 \end{cases} \label{eq:xiprelu_activation}\end{equation} xIPReLU is linearly increasing for positive inputs when $\alpha_{p > 0$ and its negative component still allow for negative-valued gradients when $\alpha_{n > 0$.}}

elements.verbatim-2411-004: import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class xIPReLU(nn.Module): def __init__(self, alpha_p_init=0.8, alpha_n_init=0.8, beta=0.5): super(xIPReLU, self).__init__() self.beta = beta self.alpha_p = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.exp(alpha_p_init) - 1)) self.alpha_n = nn.Parameter(torch.log(torch.exp(alpha_n_init) - 1)) def forward(self, x): alpha_p = F.softplus(self.alpha_p) alpha_n = F.softplus(self.alpha_n) return torch.where(x > 0, alpha_p * x * x + self.beta * x, alpha_n * x * x + self.beta * x)
